title: The Declaration of Independence Performed by Morgan Freeman, Benicio del Toro, Winona Ryder & Other Actors
description:
in History, Politics |   	July 3rd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Some suc┬Łcess┬Łful Hol┬Łly┬Łwood pro┬Łduc┬Łers spend their mon┬Łey on yachts, sports t
body:
in History, Politics |   	July 3rd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Some suc┬Łcess┬Łful Hol┬Łly┬Łwood pro┬Łduc┬Łers spend their mon┬Łey on yachts, sports teams, and Euro┬Łpean cas┬Łtles. Nor┬Łman LearŌĆÖs biggest pur┬Łchase, or at least his most famous one, was a copy of the Dec┬Łla┬Łra┬Łtion of Inde┬Łpen┬Łdence. He did not, of course, buy the kind of repro┬Łduc┬Łtion any tourist can pick up at the gift shop of a major Amer┬Łi┬Łcan his┬Łtoric site, but a ŌĆ£Dun┬Łlap broad┬Łside,ŌĆØ one of 200 or so run off by Philadel┬Łphia print┬Łer John Dun┬Łlap on the very night of July 4th, 1776. After hand┬Łing over $8.1 mil┬Łlion in exchange for the doc┬Łu┬Łment in 2001, Lear put it on tour, and it there┬Łafter made years of pub┬Łlic appear┬Łances all around the coun┬Łtry, includ┬Łing at the 2002 Olympics, Super Bowl XXXVI, and the Live 8 con┬Łcert in the city where it was made.
LearŌĆÖs pur┬Łchase also inspired a film, as it might well do for any man with his con┬Łnec┬Łtions. Co-pro┬Łduced by Lear and the late Rob Rein┬Łer, anoth┬Łer Hol┬Łly┬Łwood enthu┬Łsi┬Łast of Amer┬Łi┬Łcan pol┬Łi┬Łtics, the 2001 short at the top of the post cap┬Łtures a dra┬Łmat┬Łic read┬Łing of the Dec┬Łla┬Łra┬Łtion of Inde┬Łpen┬Łdence by a line┬Łup of big stars of the day, includ┬Łing the likes of Michael Dou┬Łglas, Winona Ryder, Edward Nor┬Łton, Ren├®e Zell┬Łweger, and Beni┬Łcio del Toro.
Their per┬Łfor┬Łmances were all shot togeth┬Łer at Inde┬Łpen┬Łdence Hall in Philadel┬Łphia by Con┬Łrad Hall, the famed cin┬Łe┬Łmatog┬Łra┬Łph┬Łer of┬ĀCool Hand Luke, Butch Cas┬Łsidy and the Sun┬Łdance Kid, Marathon Man, and┬ĀAmer┬Łi┬Łcan Beau┬Łty, on ┬ĀJuly 4, 2001.
These 25 years lat┬Łer, the film remains an invig┬Ło┬Łrat┬Łing refresh┬Łer on what the Dec┬Łla┬Łra┬Łtion of Inde┬Łpen┬Łdence actu┬Łal┬Łly says. DonŌĆÖt think of it as the next best thing to read┬Łing that hal┬Łlowed doc┬Łu┬Łment: as Mor┬Łgan Free┬Łman tells us in his intro┬Łduc┬Łtion, Thomas Jef┬Łfer┬Łson ŌĆ£intend┬Łed for the dec┬Łla┬Łra┬Łtion to be per┬Łformed, and not just read. Its words and rhythms were writ┬Łten to be spo┬Łken, in proud and defi┬Łant tones in grand pub┬Łlic places.ŌĆØ His fel┬Łlow thes┬Łpi┬Łans deliv┬Łer them with the aplomb of a coun┬Łtry that under┬Łstood itself as supreme in the world, though one does now feel a cer┬Łtain irony in their speak┬Łing in the mid-sum┬Łmer of 2001, just months before that con┬Łfi┬Łdence would be ter┬Łri┬Łbly shak┬Łen. Amer┬Łi┬Łcan his┬Łto┬Łry, it turned out, had not yet end┬Łed; even now, on the 250th anniver┬Łsary of the Unit┬Łed StatesŌĆÖ inde┬Łpen┬Łdence, it may have just bare┬Łly begun.
Relat┬Łed con┬Łtent:
Fred┬Łer┬Łick DouglassŌĆÖs Fiery 1852 Speech, ŌĆ£The Mean┬Łing of July 4th for the Negro,ŌĆØ Read by James Earl Jones
John TrumbullŌĆÖs Famous 1818 Paint┬Łing Dec┬Łla┬Łra┬Łtion of Inde┬Łpen┬Łdence Vir┬Łtu┬Łal┬Łly Defaced to Show Which Found┬Łing Fathers Owned Slaves
Read George WashingtonŌĆÖs ŌĆ£110 Rules of Civil┬Łi┬ŁtyŌĆØ: The Code of Decen┬Łcy That Guid┬Łed AmericaŌĆÖs First Pres┬Łi┬Łdent
Meet ŌĆ£Found┬Łing Moth┬ŁerŌĆØ Mary Katharine God┬Łdard, First Female Post┬Łmas┬Łter in the U.S. and Print┬Łer of the Dec┬Łla┬Łra┬Łtion of Inde┬Łpen┬Łdence
Bertrand RussellŌĆÖs 10 Com┬Łmand┬Łments for Liv┬Łing in a Healthy Democ┬Łra┬Łcy
John Wayne Recites the Pledge of Alle┬Łgiance
Based in Seoul,┬ĀCol┬Łin┬ĀMarshall┬Āwrites and broad┬Łcasts on cities, lan┬Łguage, and cul┬Łture. HeŌĆÖs the author of the newslet┬Łter┬ĀBooks on Cities┬Āas well as the books┬ĀĒĢ£ĻĄŁ ņÜöņĢĮ ĻĖłņ¦Ć┬Ā(No Sum┬Łma┬Łriz┬Łing Korea) and┬ĀKore┬Łan Newtro.┬ĀFol┬Łlow him on the social net┬Łwork for┬Łmer┬Łly known as Twit┬Łter at┬Ā@colinmarshall.

title: Meet the Syntopicon: The Ambitious Index That Tried to Organize All of Western Thought (1952)
description:
in Books, History |   	July 2nd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
Mor┬Łtimer J. Adler rose to cul┬Łtur┬Łal promi┬Łnence in the mid-twen┬Łti┬Łeth-cen┬Łtu┬Łry Unit
body:
in Books, History |   	July 2nd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
Mor┬Łtimer J. Adler rose to cul┬Łtur┬Łal promi┬Łnence in the mid-twen┬Łti┬Łeth-cen┬Łtu┬Łry Unit┬Łed States, not that a fig┬Łure like him could have done so in any oth┬Łer place or time. A hap┬Łhaz┬Łard pro┬Łfes┬Łsion┬Łal and intel┬Łlec┬Łtu┬Łal path involv┬Łing┬Ācopy-boy work at the New York┬ĀSun, night school, and an incom┬Łplete Colum┬Łbia degree even┬Łtu┬Łal┬Łly led to a fac┬Łul┬Łty posi┬Łtion teach┬Łing phi┬Łlos┬Ło┬Łphy of law at the Uni┬Łver┬Łsi┬Łty of Chica┬Łgo. In 1945, he com┬Łmenced work on what would become the Great Books of the West┬Łern World, a 54-vol┬Łume set pub┬Łlished by Ency┬Łclop├”┬Łdia Bri┬Łtan┬Łni┬Łca includ┬Łing the works of every┬Łone from Homer to Vir┬Łgil to Dar┬Łwin to Hem┬Łing┬Łway. Sold door-to-door, it became an unlike┬Łly suc┬Łcess by the ear┬Łly nine┬Łteen-six┬Łties, and for a time it was a fair┬Łly com┬Łmon, if book┬Łshelf-dom┬Łi┬Łnat┬Łing, sight in the aspi┬Łra┬Łtional homes of sub┬Łur┬Łban Amer┬Łi┬Łca.
How many of those fam┬Łi┬Łlies reg┬Łu┬Łlar┬Łly pulled their Great Books off the shelf is anoth┬Łer mat┬Łter. Despite hav┬Łing come through an inten┬Łsive process of cura┬Łtion, they could still look rather impos┬Łing as the wall of knowl┬Łedge they formed all togeth┬Łer. To this prob┬Łlem, Adler offered a char┬Łac┬Łter┬Łis┬Łti┬Łcal┬Łly ambi┬Łtious and idio┬Łsyn┬Łcrat┬Łic solu┬Łtion: a con┬Łcept-ori┬Łent┬Łed index called the Syn┬Łtopi┬Łcon ŌĆö or rather, ŌĆ£A Syn┬Łtopi┬Łcon.ŌĆØ
ŌĆ£He believed these two vol┬Łumes to be just the ŌĆśassis┬ŁtanceŌĆÖ that the aver┬Łage man need┬Łed to dig into the books that formed West┬Łern Civ┬Łi┬Łliza┬Łtion,ŌĆØ writes Jonathan White, an alum┬Łnus of the sim┬Łi┬Łlar┬Łly West┬Łern canon-based St. JohnŌĆÖs Col┬Łlege. They┬ĀŌĆ£com┬Łprised an exhaus┬Łtive cat┬Ła┬Łlogue of each time one of the 102 ŌĆśGreat Ideas of West┬Łern Civ┬Łi┬Łliza┬ŁtionŌĆÖ was men┬Łtioned in the 431 ŌĆśGreat BooksŌĆÖ enshrined in Bri┬Łtan┬Łni┬ŁcaŌĆÖs col┬Łlec┬Łtion.ŌĆØ

Good and evil, log┬Łic and love, plea┬Łsure and pain, uni┬Łver┬Łsal and par┬Łtic┬Łu┬Łlar: all the big ideas, at least as Adler defined them, were there in A Syn┬Łtopi┬Łcon. Cus┬Łtomers report┬Łed┬Łly found it unwieldy, but the notion behind it holds out a cer┬Łtain appeal still today. ItŌĆÖs even inspired the launch of Syntopi.com, a dig┬Łi┬Łtal suc┬Łces┬Łsor that enables you to nav┬Łi┬Łgate ŌĆ£the Great Con┬Łver┬Łsa┬ŁtionŌĆØ in a vari┬Łety of ways includ┬Łing a 3D visu┬Łal┬Łiza┬Łtion and a per┬Łson┬Łal cur┬Łricu┬Łlum-cre┬Łation tool. The Great Books of the West┬Łern WorldŌĆÖs mid-cen┬Łtu┬Łry read┬Łers ŌĆö pro┬Łfes┬Łsion┬Łals and busi┬Łness┬Łmen look┬Łing to fill the gaps in their gen┬Łer┬Łal knowl┬Łedge, vet┬Łer┬Łans ready to learn more after their G.I. Bill-fund┬Łed col┬Łlege edu┬Łca┬Łtion, house┬Łwives hop┬Łing to get a han┬Łdle on what intel┬Łli┬Łgent peo┬Łple were sup┬Łposed to know about ŌĆö could have had fun with it. And we could ben┬Łe┬Łfit, no doubt, from redis┬Łcov┬Łer┬Łing a lit┬Łtle of their earnest┬Łly self-improv┬Łing spir┬Łit our┬Łselves. You can view an edi┬Łtion of A Syn┬Łtopi┬Łcon on the Inter┬Łnet Archive, or this site.
Relat┬Łed Con┬Łtent:
The West┬Łern Canon: From Homer to Mil┬Łton (Free Course)
Harold Bloom Cre┬Łates a Mas┬Łsive List of Works in The ŌĆ£West┬Łern CanonŌĆØ: Read Many of the Books Free Online
The West┬Łern Tra┬Łdi┬Łtion by Eugen Weber: 52 Video Lec┬Łtures
Great Big Ideas: Free Course Fea┬Łtures Top Thinkers Tack┬Łling the WorldŌĆÖs Most Impor┬Łtant Ideas in 12 Lec┬Łtures
48 Ani┬Łmat┬Łed Videos Explain the His┬Łto┬Łry of Ideas: From Aris┬Łto┬Łtle to Sartre
The 10 Great┬Łest Books Ever, Accord┬Łing to 125 Top Authors (Down┬Łload Them for Free)
Based in Seoul,┬ĀCol┬Łin┬ĀMarshall┬Āwrites and broad┬Łcasts on cities, lan┬Łguage, and cul┬Łture. HeŌĆÖs the author of the newslet┬Łter┬ĀBooks on Cities┬Āas well as the books┬ĀĒĢ£ĻĄŁ ņÜöņĢĮ ĻĖłņ¦Ć┬Ā(No Sum┬Łma┬Łriz┬Łing Korea) and┬ĀKore┬Łan Newtro.┬ĀFol┬Łlow him on the social net┬Łwork for┬Łmer┬Łly known as Twit┬Łter at┬Ā@colinmarshall.

title: Katherine Wolkoff Meditates on Absence in Her Tender Photos of Deer Beds
description: The last few mornings, as IŌĆÖve walked with my dog up the ravine behind my house, two fawns seem to bound of thin air, racing in unison through the
body:
The last few mornings, as IŌĆÖve walked with my dog up the ravine behind my house, two fawns seem to bound of thin air, racing in unison through the trees until far enough way that they stop, stare, and wait for us to pass. ItŌĆÖs not uncommon to see several does grazing in the same woods, and IŌĆÖve always wondered where they sleep. Photographer Katherine Wolkoff followed a similar curiosity as she traversed the grassy meadows of Block Island, which sits a few miles off the coast of Rhode Island, for her series Deer Beds.
Flattened by lean cervid bodies, tall grasses reveal the areas where deer bed down. They donŌĆÖt typically sleep in the same place every single night, but a home range area may have several spots that they return to repeatedly. Wolkoff prints the images at nearly life size, focusing directly on the nest-like spaces in intimate, horizonless meditations on comfort, presence, care, and resilience.

When the series was first exhibited, critic Eva Diaz noted in Artforum that ŌĆ£The prevailing metaphor of photography is that of the hunt. Photographers shoot, even stalk, their subjects; in the case of Katherine WolkoffŌĆÖs work, the absence of ŌĆśpreyŌĆÖ itself becomes the subject of the project.ŌĆØ Sometimes during a walk, the artist encounters deer nestled in the grass and they dart away, startled. Other times, the beds are already empty.
ŌĆ£My mother, a science teacher, first mentioned deer beds to me, and I began walking the fields, following deer paths to find them,ŌĆØ Wolkoff tells Colossal. ŌĆ£That solitary, meditative search is still central to how I work today.ŌĆØ Broadly, her work focuses on the natural world in the Anthropocene, plumbing the relationship between humans and the land in light of the ongoing climate crisis.
The artist is currently finishing a book of pinhole photographs taken from the perspective of migrating birds on Block Island. ŌĆ£The resulting pictures have a blurred, frantic quality that I think of as visualizing the birdsŌĆÖ depletion: the chaos of an animal pushed to its limits over open water and unfamiliar coastline, flying through the night with no guarantee of where it will land or whether it will survive the crossing,ŌĆØ she says. Some of these works will be part of a forthcoming exhibition centered around the Atlantic Flyway at Benrubi Gallery next spring.

schema.headline: Katherine Wolkoff Meditates on Absence in Her Tender Photos of Deer Beds

title: Guimi You's Atmospheric Oil Paintings Delve into Memory, Introspection, and Rediscovery
description: Whether itŌĆÖs the atmosphere casting a haze or the fuzziness of memories and dreams, Guimi YouŌĆÖs lush paintings have an aura of wistfulness and qui
body:
Whether itŌĆÖs the atmosphere casting a haze or the fuzziness of memories and dreams, Guimi YouŌĆÖs lush paintings have an aura of wistfulness and quietude. The Seoul-based artist creates dreamy oil compositions that tap into personal experience, passing time, and how one gains perspective and reevaluates their needs or desires as they go through life.
YouŌĆÖs canvases are infused with elements of still life and landscape traditions, where anonymous protagonists reflect quietly in a garden, pause in a golden meadow, or stroll through a park in the rain. Cerulean shadows complement the magenta jacket of a woman strolling with her dog along a stream in ŌĆ£Spring Walk,ŌĆØ and a woman sits down at an easel in an open doorway in ŌĆ£Painting, Again.ŌĆØ
ŌĆ£Park in the DrizzleŌĆØ (2026), oil on linen, 46 x 36 inches. Photo by Ian Yang
Several of the works seen here are included in YouŌĆÖs current solo exhibition, When the Sun Shines Again at Lehmann Maupin, a meditation on rediscovery and beginning again. ŌĆ£Conceived as a tribute to those who have returned to creating after time away, the works unfold through a recurring image of sunlight, an enduring symbol of clarity, renewal, and hope,ŌĆØ the gallery says.
The figures sometimes appear to blend into the backgrounds like secondary characters, while the spaces filled with objects, contrasting light, and portals take center stage. Individual faces are often obscured or shadowy, introspectively looking down or away. Some appear lost in thought, while others concentrate on a creative task, such as throwing pottery on a wheel or putting a brush to canvas. There is a somberness tinged with hope as You taps into the courage required to start anew.
The artistŌĆÖs approach, the gallery says, ŌĆ£synthesizes East Asian pictorial traditions of evocation and atmospheric transparency with Western lineages such as Romanticism and Surrealism, positioning landscape not as an escapist subject, but as a space for reflecting on memory, subjectivity, and how we situate ourselves within the world.ŌĆØ
When the Sun Shines Again continues through August 14 in New York City.
ŌĆ£Painting, AgainŌĆØ (2026). oil on linen, 65 x 81 7/8 x 1 1/8 inches. Photo by JeongKyun Goh
ŌĆ£Spring WalkŌĆØ (2026), oil on linen, 65 x 81 7/8 x 1 1/8 inches. Photo by JeongKyun Goh
ŌĆ£PauseŌĆØ (2025), oil on linen, 65 x 80 inches. Photo by OnArt Studio
ŌĆ£The Shape of SilenceŌĆØ (2026), oil on linen, 81 7/8 x 65 x 1 1/8 inches. Photo by JeongKyun Goh
ŌĆ£Let the Sunlight InŌĆØ (2026), oil on linen, 65 x 79 7/8 x 1 1/8 inches. Photo by JeongKyun Goh
ŌĆ£GoldenŌĆØ (2026), oil on linen, 74 x 59 7/8 x 1 1/8 inches. Photo by JeongKyun Goh
ŌĆ£Deep in the YellowŌĆØ (2025), oil on linen, 63 7/8 x 44 1/8 inches. Photo by OnArt Studio

title: What Does the Statue of Liberty Stand For?
description:
Feature
                As artists like Amy Sherald, Marta Minuj├Łn, and Faith Ringgold remind us, the monument is far from a neu
body:
Feature
                As artists like Amy Sherald, Marta Minuj├Łn, and Faith Ringgold remind us, the monument is far from a neutral symbol of so-called American values.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            A visitor with Amy SheraldŌĆÖs ŌĆ£Trans Forming LibertyŌĆØ (2024) at the Whitney Museum of American Art (photo Hrag Vartanian/Hyperallergic)
    
        
            Last July, Amy Sherald announced that she was canceling the National Portrait Gallery stop of her solo exhibition American Sublime because of concerns that the Smithsonian Institution had attempted to censor her painting of a Black trans woman, Arew├Ā Basit, posing as the Statue of Liberty.Lindsey Halligan, a special assistant to President Donald Trump, responded to SheraldŌĆÖs decision with satisfaction. ŌĆ£The Statue of Liberty is not an abstract canvas for political expression ŌĆö it is a revered and solemn symbol of freedom, inspiration, and national unity that defines the American spirit,ŌĆØ she said in a statement at the time.But all national symbols, whether flags, statues, or personifications of founding ideals, are precisely that: ŌĆ£abstract canvases for political expression.ŌĆØ Their entire purpose is to function as a screen onto which political ideals can be projected. And, as the political winds shift, so too do their meanings.Contrary to HalliganŌĆÖs statement, the Statue of Liberty has never been a stable symbol of ŌĆ£freedom, inspiration, and national unity.ŌĆØ
                  
                      
                        Feature
                      
                      
                        Statue of Liberty
                      
                      
                        Faith Ringgold
                      
                      
                        Amy Sherald

schema.headline: What Does the Statue of Liberty Stand For?

title: The Hudson River SchoolŌĆÖs American Apocalypse
description:
Opinion
                One of the United StatesŌĆÖs first major art movements registered anxieties about industrialization, empir
body:
Opinion
                One of the United StatesŌĆÖs first major art movements registered anxieties about industrialization, empire, and environmental ruin.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            Frederic Edwin Church, "Our Banner in the Sky" (1861), oil on paper (photo public domain CC0 via Wikimedia Commons)
    
        
            By the standards of geological epochs, Kaaterskill Falls in Upstate New York is positively youthful. Generated by melting glacial runoff eroding the sandstone and shale at the foot of the CatskillsŌĆÖ South Mountain during the middle Pleistocene ŌĆö a spritely 130,000 years ago ŌĆö Kaaterskill is a spectacular two-stage waterfall that seems to almost bounce down its 260-foot (~79-meter) height. Two centuries ago, that cascading waterfall would inspire a 25-year-old engraver born in Lancashire, England, who wanted to forge a new American aesthetics. Thomas ColeŌĆÖs ŌĆ£Kaaterskill FallsŌĆØ of 1826, on view at the Wadsworth Atheneum Museum of Art in Hartford, Connecticut, heralded that new movement.Painted at his nearby Cedar Grove studio, ŌĆ£Kaaterskill FallsŌĆØ turned the portraiture that had marked late colonial and early Republican American art in the mode of Gilbert Stuart and Rembrandt Peale to the wall. Where that work was staid, decorous, classical, and European, this adopted American made a declaration of independence from the art academies of London and Paris. Seemingly painted from the perspective of the rock-shelter behind the falls itself, the work depicts powerful water flowing into the creek below, framed by autumnal leaves of Eastern Hemlocks, Northern Red Oaks, Sugar Maples, and American Beeches rendered in green, red, orange, and brown. Through the mist and haze of the water droplets, the sun drops into a bleeding dusk, nightfall moving in with the black clouds. ŌĆ£The passion caused by the great and sublime in nature, when those causes operate most powerfully, is astonishment,ŌĆØ wrote the Anglo-Irish philosopher Edmund Burke in A Philosophical Enquiry into the Origin of our Ideas of the Sublime and Beautiful from 1757. A clarion call to the emerging Romantic movement, BurkeŌĆÖs claims about sublimity were first truly embodied in the American landscape, the only patrimony the young Republic had yet to offer.┬Ā
                  
                      
                        Opinion
                      
                      
                        Hudson River School
                      
                      
                        USA 250

schema.headline: The Hudson River SchoolŌĆÖs American Apocalypse

title: 10 Art Shows to See in Los Angeles This July
description:
Guide
                Marc KreiselŌĆÖs secret art practice, Gwyneth BulawskyŌĆÖs queer and trans landscapes, Barbara CarrascoŌĆÖs life
body:
Guide
                Marc KreiselŌĆÖs secret art practice, Gwyneth BulawskyŌĆÖs queer and trans landscapes, Barbara CarrascoŌĆÖs lifelong muralism, and more.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            Left to right: Kimberly Robertson (Mvskoke), ŌĆ£Deer Woman (pink),ŌĆØ ŌĆ£Deer Woman (blue),ŌĆØ ŌĆ£Deer Woman (purple),ŌĆØ and ŌĆ£Deer Woman (peach)ŌĆØ (all 2024), beaded tapestries of metal hangers, plastic and wood beads, acrylic paint, and lipstick knives (photo Brica Wilcox, courtesy FCCW)
    
        
            This July, Los Angeles is rife with exhibitions that confront present-day challenges and conflicts in ways that are both pragmatic and poetic. Radical Kinship features eight women artists whose practices highlight informal communal networks, while Eva Aguila looks at the essential but contentious roles played by Mexican workers in the United States, framed by her own familial legacy. A career-spanning show on beloved Angeleno artist Barbara Carrasco at Charlie James Gallery showcases her lifelong synthesis of art and activism. And at Cevera Yoon, Jaime Pattison and Maura Brewer adapt the language and tools of the information economy to create haunting soundscapes and geometric abstractions.Vincent Ramos: Notice of Demolition or The Horror-Vacui Heavy Haunts House Band for The Haunted House┬Āas-is, 1133 Venice Boulevard, Pico-Union, Los AngelesThrough July 18Vincent Ramos, ŌĆ£Pachuco Cadaver: You Can Kill a Revolutionary but You CanŌĆÖt Kill the RevolutionŌĆØ (2022ŌĆō26) (photo Yubo Dong, courtesy as-is)By mining popular media and material artifacts, artist Vincent Ramos creates assemblages and collages that examine the affinities and frictions between Mexican-American culture and mainstream American culture as a whole. His evocative patchworks often pay homage to Latine performers or artists who achieved crossover success, such as Anthony Quinn (born Manuel Antonio Rodolfo Quinn Oaxaca in Chihuahua) and Linda Ronstadt, as well as Herb Albert, a Jewish musician raised in Boyle Heights who helped introduce Latin American music to White audiences. Witty and insightful, RamosŌĆÖs poetic pastiches wade through the muddy hinterlands of hybridity, raising questions about which narratives are adopted, or excluded, from the mainstream.Barbara Carrasco: On The EdgeCharlie James Gallery, 969 Chung King Road, Chinatown, Los AngelesThrough July 18Barbara Carrasco, "Detained Child #3" (2026), acrylic on canvas (photo Yubo Dong / ofstudio, courtesy the artist and Charlie James Gallery)Throughout her five-decade career, Barbara Carrasco has been a leading voice in the space between art and activism, from her role as a muralist with the United Farm Workers and her once-censored mural ŌĆ£L.A. History: A Mexican PerspectiveŌĆØ (1981), now on view at the cityŌĆÖs Natural History Museum, to bold, graphic portraits of Civil Rights icons such as Martin Luther King Jr. and Dolores Huerta. On the Edge presents new paintings of children detained under the USŌĆÖs increasingly draconian immigration policies, each pictured with a butterfly, a hopeful symbol of liberation. The exhibition also features a selection of CarrascoŌĆÖs historical works, highlighting her poignant juxtapositions of the personal and political.Erotic Terrains┬ĀThe Loved One, 1634 West Temple Street, Historic Filipinotown, Los AngelesThrough July 24Wyatt Mills, "Hard Exterior" (2026), oil on canvas (image courtesy Wienholt Projects and Hat & Beard)The Loved One is a new art bookshop and gallery co-founded by publisher Hat & Beard Press and curatorial platform Wienholt Projects. (Art-savvy Angelenos may remember the building as the site of the Enzo Art Fair earlier this year.) Its inaugural exhibition, Erotic Terrains, is a group show primarily featuring LA-based artists who explore liminal spaces and the uncanny through painting, sculpture, photography, and collage. Highlights include Wyatt MillsŌĆÖs figurative surrealism, Alex McAdooŌĆÖs suburban psychedelia, and Francesca GabbianiŌĆÖs cut-paper palm tree on fire in ŌĆ£Mutation XLVIŌĆØ (2023).
